speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Kendrick and Austin took me up in the cab. Because I never did ride with Rich.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He was a mason.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Pardon me?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He was a mason. Ernie. Ernie was a mason. I used to use 10 logs a while down here. It happened that one time he was down here on a work trade. I had a work trade here and he was running that. When he was here in Kendrick on that work trade, he used 10 logs. Then he got on the main line and then he got he finished up on the full can of Lewis. He finished up his run.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Did you ask him questions?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, oh yeah I got the questions we didn't we ran out of time on last time. I've kind of forgotten where it was. I was thinking about the train. Did people use the train a lot more?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They used to have when they first built this train here they had three trains down and three trains up a day. Then they cut it down to four trains. Two down two up. And then on up to last they had one down and one back. It just comes down turn around a little to go back and it got so it wasn't paid. No train taught.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well a train runs out there occasionally I assume lately.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Not a passenger train? No. It's in crates. It's all crates. Oh yeah there was six passenger trains. Here and besides the crates.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well if you wanted to go somewhere would you go down to Kendrick and catch it out of there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You used to be down in Brackert Canyon. Well I never did that but I guess it was college. Stop there at Bovard. That's a double track. It's a stop you know. You go to Bovard and they stop and pick you up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Where's Bovard?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well they tried down you know where I'm at from the junior years. They're getting all the hill from him right now. Double track. Trains pass there. You got a telephone in a little house down there and that's Bovard. If you want to get on the train and go to Kendrick and instead of going to Kendrick you just cut down over the hill or foot. You'd be there in the train there and they'd stop and pick you up. And let you off. They wouldn't do it now I'm sure. They're too independent now. No everybody went on the train. We didn't have buses. Greyhound wasn't thought of. The buses and the trucks that put the railroad out of business. That's kind of a shame too. Good roads. Now the airplanes and everything. Tack pairs are building the roads with the top of that trucker. Yep. And the buses. That's one thing the railroad company when they come in here they bulk their right away. The government give them a lot of land for their right away. They built their own depots and tracks and stuff when they charge you to ride on it. An airplane you've got to build an airport before they land to pick you up. That's the truth. Oh no. Yes sir. That's always stuck in my craw. Why we had to build an airport for an aircraft outfit to land to pick you up. The railroaders didn't ask for it. No. No sir.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They told me it would be a short time for Lewis to be on the head. They closed them out just as they can. The government has helped the COCI Airlines. I just don't know how many million that gave them. Well they're priming the pumps all the time for them. Now Lewis didn't keep taking flights off every little while. They got another flight off. And they ain't going to be very long. Moscow does nothing stop there except just a little cascade outfit and lots of lollipops yet. And Lewis is getting trimmed down. The DC-9 that morning flight that comes through there. And when we come back from Seattle on from Alaska last fall it's taken off. And they're just going to gradually keep it taking it off. They don't like to stop a little place like that. So they get the regional airport over here and I don't think I doubt whether Pullman and Moscow and those, and they want to rule everything and you want everything and Lewis to them but they just want to face it. Because if they don't they ain't going to have nothing. It's an aircraft outfit that's just going to pull out and leave them sitting there high and dry.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well what if they have a good airport in Lewis and don't they?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yes they do.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Don't jet land there and take them?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: There's a big Nines land there. Moscow's got an airport out here that's a thousand feet long with a runway. There's a thousand feet longer than one in Lewis and they can't get a DC-9 that's like a lander.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Not enough anchor? Too close to Lewis?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They don't hardly get off the ground in Lewis until they're stopping at Moscow. We went to Seattle last fall and by and gala they take east and they soak around over the east side of the orchards and come around over halfway crit and look down to see Lewis and around those grounds and TFI and all of them down there. And by the time you, well before you get to the top of the hill, you're up quite a ways. By the time you get to the top of the hill, you'll be down here on the right hand there and see that's Moscow and Pullman. But they don't hardly get off the ground until they get land in the get wall and they don't like to do that. You see?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It's too close together.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: This is as easy as rolling.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I can understand that but do you think that the Cock-eyed Central Airport up there is going to be the thing?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: What does that... Well, there's times they're not going to be able to use it. It's... In the winter time when you get blizzards and fog and stuff, by the gala, I don't see how they're going to be able to use it. I just don't see it since both towns have gone on airports. Of course, like you say, the big planes won't land in Moscow. No, they're not landing there at all. They quit it. Years old boy. But Lewis and Bullheaded and they want everything down there and they're going to wind up not having anything. Because the big airplane companies, they just won't stop the place like this. They claim the boarding's in it now. Well, there's too many people that used to get on at Lewis from Moscow and Pullman and it just takes them a few minutes longer to run Spokane. They want to go east. They go get in a Congo Spokane. Pullman, Moscow. That's what they tell me they're doing and it sounds reasonable. They don't go down to the top around as much. That's right. They got a good road to Spokane. Take that off to look fairly. So, they're going to leave Moscow and be in Spokane an hour and a half. No one ever had too fast either. It's only 82 miles in Colfax. They drive 67 miles an hour. They're out there a little bit. You know, if they build a decent road from Lewis into Moscow, which would be very simple to do, that wasn't a good one to do a build. I sure don't approve of that. They come right up to the Cockhide Canyon and take up one of those drugs. They got a natural. They got a natural. They come right up halfway quick until they get to that north fork of the Highway Creek and it comes on right at the old Archibald and the Erickson place and it's right south of the Genesee. There'd just be one main turn in the road. That's where they'd turn up the north fork of the Highway Creek and they'd go straight to the old one. It wouldn't be very far too. And the Noah would be a real water grade. The Lewiston wants everything. See, it wouldn't take off down below us. They'd take off up there by halfway quick. The Lewiston's so afraid that somebody's going to take off and not take off and they won't take off right out there but done quick. So this piece showed in the tribute a while back. Take off right there, you know.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: But I think the halfway is the best grade.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I think, come on up, come on up to the next, that, what do they call that, little bit of dark brick?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, it's coyote grade. Well, yeah, but up here, a little, they'd go up a little dark brick.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: A little polish?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, a little polish.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They'd come out right up there to, well, oh, what's the name of that little settlement over there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, it'd be pertinent to Cornwall. It'd be right at Cornwall. Well, Cornwall and Towall.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It wouldn't, the driest from Lewiston to Moscow wouldn't amount to nothing. Well, if you want to really get a good road that you wouldn't have to come up to Giaty, you could take up a little pot batch. It's on the other side of Fix Ridge. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It comes out down to the Conford Rocks who else?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Heck, that comes out up there east of, east of Genesee at Thorn Creek. Thorn Creek Schoolhouse in that valley of Lutheran, through to Genesee, is on the head of that creek. I think that's just a smooth water grade right up there. I think that's the place for it to go. Yeah, but Lewiston wants everything to come out of Lewiston. They're going to, they're going to kick themselves on this airplane business by not getting in and playing ballroom. But some of these days, they're just going about, well, they know in three years, they don't have an airport. They're done. They've told them. They're taking off flights all the time. The more they take off, the more the less, the more things they have, people just get in their car and go, oh, yeah. Lewiston's always been pretty jealous of having things to do with it. Well, they always want everything. They've got a bunch of businessmen down there that don't see much further than the end of their nose. Well, when Lewiston used to be the capital of Idaho, you know, there was a big fight there to try to take, try to take the capital away.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They fought for a couple years.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And somebody old stowed it.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They kicked themselves ever since because we got the University of Idaho up here. Oh, yeah, Southern Idaho thinks that's true. They've got two big schools down there. Do you remember the... They made a trade, you know. How we come to get the university. And boys, they got the penitentiary. You look back from years, as the Indians said years ago in history, way back, and that... We had a choice. They'd give us a choice of taking the University of the Penitentiary. We took the university and they took the pen and they kicked their ass over something. They saw what a mistake they made. Well, I've always got a figure that maybe the Coeur d 'Alene mines had a lot to do with that way in the university. Well, the Coeur d 'Alene mines is one of us. Their taxes don't hurt to say to Idaho anything. No. Do you remember the... Them people down there don't care anything about North Idaho.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you remember the big fight years ago about trying to make the state out of North Idaho, Eastern Washington?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I remember one time that they was going to take the University of Idaho away from us and they liked one vote. And they kissed one vote of Gippett and the legislature. There was talk one time of cutting off the northern part of Idaho and the eastern part of Washington and making a state out of it. On the western part of Montana.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: But Montana didn't do anything to that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You take the western part of Montana... This side, it'd be this side of the divide. Yeah, the divide was supposed to have been the one. And then Dan Gum's Saveras they had that run that line through from down there from Salmon City. You know, you come up there and then you've got to go this way, off this way. And then Dan Gum's Saveras was drunk for days and they kept a bearing too much on the left. And when they got up there in North Idaho, they'd boast so much on the left, they were putting her over to Washington. They were supposed to follow up the Continental Divide. The Continental Divide is just east of Butte. That's the Continental Divide. And they were way old. And they never changed it. They let it stand. That's where it's supposed to be. You look back in your old history and that's where the Saveras got followed up. That's where they were called to go. Yeah. And they didn't follow the by. The story I heard was that they got confused between the Bitter Roots and the Rockies. And they ended up running along the Bitter Roots. And just kept going off history to the left all the time. No, western Montana was intended to...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, the Bitter Roots did the divide now, didn't it, no?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It's a divide between here and Montana. Yeah. But the Continental Divide is just east of Butte.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Right.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: The Continental Divide was supposed to be. I thought it was supposed to be a big square state. That's right.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Now, Spokane, the money of the capital and the earnings of the Coeur d 'Alene mines is what built Spokane. Mm-hmm. And the water power from the ideal stream is what built Spokane. In Spokane, eastern Washington has the same problems as we have again in South Idaho. Yeah. And they get just what western Washington wants them to have.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Because they've got the population over there, see?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They just give them the crumbs over here. That's just what we get up here. Whatever happened to that movement...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Pardon? Whatever happened to that movement to make a new state?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't know. It died just like it was born, I guess. It was going to call it Lincoln. And nothing ever comes out. Yeah, they even had a name picked out for you. No, you take eastern Washington. Just take in Spokane.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And western Montana there. Montana's a big state. You get on the railroad and see how long it takes you to cross that state. I've driven across it in the car. It's a long way to cross. You bet of this. You're not a kid. I never bet across the northern south, but I bet across the east and west. You travel all the way to Chicago on the north coast of the limited. We left Spokane in the morning. Stayed all night in the hotel in Spokane. And we got on at 9 o 'clock, or 9-5, so I entered it in just a few minutes before or after 9 o 'clock. But the NPD pulled out of Spokane and we rode all day, all that night, and got up next morning with over Bamao City. We're still in Montana. That old train clips right along, they don't stop there by the kitchen coop. They clip right along 60 miles an hour. We're still in Montana. I know we're just getting daylight when we went to mile city. That's a long way across Montana. Mile's a specific class of class. Yes, it is.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: We didn't talk much last time about depression. I know in the 1893 depression, somebody told me that a lot of the early homesteaders were busted out by that depression and had someone that went down to the Lapwai country on there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did most people in the 30s depression manage to hold on to their places?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: A lot of people forced to move then too. I don't think so. Our folks both held on to there. We were just poor in Joe Paterky, but they held on. Dad and mother was here, sold wheat for 22 cents a bushel and hauled it from here to Moscow with harp here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That 93? Was that 93?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did they ever talk about the difference between the two different depressions? The difference between the two?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yes. If they did, I don't remember. That was a terrible one, I'll tell you.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The first one or the second one?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: The first one. The second one was terrible. I know what that was. But the first one was a real bad one. You can imagine selling wheat for 22 cents a bushel and hauled it to Moscow. I dare say they had that much in it by the time they got it out there. On a dirt road. On a dirt road, no bottom to the road. It was all on a dirt road. A damn poor dirt road at that. But they had to haul it and sell it in order to pay what bills they could pay. The Raishmen had to be paid. A lot of time they didn't get paid. Well, there were people that went busted, I'm sure of that. There was lots of them. My folks were one of the few that cut their grain harvest.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: My folk, Sam, was one of the few that cut the grain in before rain. Yeah. They had a little company in the trash machine here, and he was lucky. It only had just a few acres. I don't believe... But he got it fresh before it started raining. I don't remember. At that time, Dad and Uncle Harris were farming together, and they were heading. And their wheat was probably all in the stack, I don't know. I don't know whether it was in the stack or not. But I think it was. One old dad said by the way, he said it just rained and rained and rained. He said they had a sack pile. It got their trash and they had it covered. They said it took four horses on a wagon to pull ten sacks out of the field. He said he was going to Britain at the hub. Gets out of the field. And hauled it in, stored it in the barn. And they had a clue, but there's a lot of them that never saved the Colonel. A lot of things. We think by the way, we have a tuff, but I tell you, they really had it run.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Was that about...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Is that about the horse thing it had to go through, or was it harder in the first few years?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Was it harder to kind of get through a depression, or was it harder to... Oh, people on this second depression we had more than we were used to more. We all had cars. Mr. Roosevelt, when he took it over from Hoover, we had cars and better. They were just getting a little better roads. They didn't have much rock on the roads. They never got any rock on the road around here until we got the old WPA. That's where we got this coarse rock on these roads here. But people were better off on the second one here than they were in that 93.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, in the 93?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, a little gentleman came here and they didn't have anything when they got here. People on the Roosevelt Depression, they had better homes, better buildings. Most everybody had a car. Sometimes it wasn't a first class car, but it gets you there and back. I think our car... It wasn't nothing like you were in the 93, I don't think. Our car set in the shed for, I think, two years. I couldn't even afford to buy a damn for it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, were you still using horses? Were you using horses in the 30s?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah, horses and mules. You bet you used horses. I've heard mules are pretty hard to handle. I've heard mules are pretty hard to handle. Oh, no, no, no. That's a mistaken idea. I love mules. He worked them for years.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You think they're easier to handle than a horse?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I liked them better than horses. I really did. One thing they were famous at is pound beans. They were famous at clown beans. Them long legs and, boy, they could just walk a horse to death. They took longer steps. They did not walk a horse. Another thing I liked about them, they all seemed to be even. Your horse team always had a damn slow one or something faster. The mules, they were all gated about the same. Another thing, a mule wouldn't step on a bean. No, a mule would walk on a bean. A bean or a corner would be plowing the garden or anything like that. I don't know why, but they just didn't step on stuff. Now, how they could tell, I don't know. A damn horse, you know, he would drag his feet. He would always be able to step on his feet and drag it out of the ground, you know. But a mule would walk a horse to death. They're faster along the way than they just did on the other side. A mule, we used to drill a lot with all the drillers. The mules, after I got the mule, they go pretty slow up the hill. But boy, when they got on top, they'd stretch right out. A mule was smart, but they didn't want to take care of themselves. They just ploddled on up the hill and they'd get out on top and they just stepped right out. They didn't have to encourage them. Was there an answer?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, are mules, when they get an idea in their head, are they stubborn? Is everybody says they aren't?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, they're... Mules are stubborn, yeah, but I don't know.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That never bothered me. And I didn't have any trouble getting along with them. Maybe it's because I was just stubborn.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I used to think lots of new woodies, you know, that's all they ever find. I didn't love mules. Sam Bigum had them. Yeah, Sam Bigum had them.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Remember that old red stub you used to have?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. You'd bring one of them on away. That was really a tall mule. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And he used to work on the bong wagon and he had a big old black mare on one side and then he had that little mable mare, you remember, on the other side. That old stub, he could pull that wagon. He could pull the other two together. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Do you think...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: The depression was a time when a lot of the farms started changing from small farms to big farms?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, that happened. I'm going to just say that happened about... 1929 or 1930. That's when the farms started getting bigger. Well, it got to the point that the times were so tough and early... late 1929 and the early 1930s, the times were so tough that you just couldn't make it on small farms. And another thing, too. Lots of times the kids went off to school and when they got to school they didn't come back to the farm. They found out that they could make a living easier than on the farm. And they followed a half... Where he was farming 40 years to be people living on the ridge here, they made a living on 40 acres of ground, 80 acres. Raise the family. That's right. And they found out pretty soon one guy had 80 acres sold out to another guy that had 160. And that's the way it went. That movement, I think, started about the time to have both the antling places. 1929.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It used to be pretty unusual for... in the early days for a boy to leave the farm that most people usually stay in the farm.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, us guys here, all of them stayed on the ridge. But there was a lot of them by the way. They'd go off to school and a large percent of them never come back. They'd learn about education, got a better job with less work. But nowadays, there's quite a few of these country boys go to school and come back and farm. They got the lean on. Well, back in 1932, you could hire a man for a dollar a day and you could get a damn good man for a dollar and a half. Wasn't it really a new business? Best man I ever had on the place, I paid a dollar and a half.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: But I worked for it like, what do you got on there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: For 39 cents. 59 cents for a good barrel over all the copper rivets in the corners. Gee, a coat now is terrible.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: A coat. You take a coat now and just wear it around the place. Yeah. I was out to Moscow the other day and I thought I'd get me a Levi. I wanted to find out where Jack got that coat you got. But they didn't have my size. You know, with little bitty ones. I mean, just the same as 15 bucks. That didn't include the tax.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You can buy a pair of copper all of them and see what you pay for them. Oh, yeah. No, you can buy a good pair. A good pair of overalls for 59 cents. A good work shirt. 39 cents. Two and a half for a pair of shoes. Send the shares over to my government works. That's where we're all in our shoes. And it wasn't the right way to do it. That's why my big toes are both crooked. You get to get a pair of shoes right off and then when you did, you're particularly good. I mean, if you get your foot in it, you're your brother. He's pretty awesome. Harry, I'm going to have to put a little head feet. Charlie sent off mail order out and getting Sarah's shoes and didn't fit for him.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Martin Long told me about a farmer off of, well, I think it was off of this ridge. Who used to, he didn't want to bring his kids into the store because he didn't want, I guess he didn't want to see a bunch of things they wanted to get and pestering him about them. Probably. So he used to bring in a stick for each kid's foot. I guess every fall when he got his property, he'd bring in a stick for each kid's foot and shove him in until he found the right size shoe. Another thing in the big family like that, they could hand him down too. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Kids, you know, by the way, wear a fair shoe and hand him down to his younger brother or sister. Under. They wore everything out on that day.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: People in them, they were happy at that. Oh, yeah. Sometimes I think they might be just as happy or more happy than they are now. People nowadays are used to so much and have so much that they get bored if they don't have everything plush. One thing, we didn't have any income tax to pay there. You didn't have no income. Well, there was no income tax.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: When did they start this income tax business?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, we were paying income tax quite a while before me and my daughter married. It must have been in the early 20s. It must have been.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did the co-ops come in out here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, we've had some started pretty early, but they didn't make it. They went belly up. But the first co-op that I remember around here that started was the Kenry Groschweil. Oh, no. And then that Creamery.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Remember that Creamery down here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And then was that Farmer's Hardware.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Farmer's Hardware wasn't the Farmer's Union?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They were the first ones and then they went belly up. That's on the ridge here. I was going to school over there on the ridge when it was going up.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: The Rochstel was first, as I remember. Right. And the Farmer's Hardware, that was a co-op, you know, there. The Comfort Kitchen was, and Granddad had stock in it. And then that Creamery, remember, that was not the key we got there. They had a co-op Creamery. It went belly up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how did the co-ops are so big today? How did they get started around here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I don't know. The worst element is with a co-op is to get a manager that's honest enough that he won't bleed all the profits out and stick it in his own pocket. That's what happens a lot of times. Get some crooked guy in there. A small co-op is easy to do. For a bigger cooperation, it wouldn't be easy. These little ones, you know, by the way, you get the wrong man in there, you know, why he could just lead it to death. And that happened.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Around here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: What's the name of that co-op there in Moscow?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

Between where he lived and up towards where Helen lived and all that. He used to be pretty timber to country. Yeah. In there. Chase Skyles was in there a couple of times. Brown Wiley is there. Well, everybody, they didn't have anything. They didn't have any money, but they were happy. They'd help the other guy out. They were neighborly, good people. Good-hearted. Well, in reality, they were all in it together. True. They were all just alive.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Mm-hmm. That's what they did. Yeah, just like the bush. They could get land for, well, if they could get a homestead. My dad was about the only guy, I guess, that didn't get a homestead out here. He had to buy his off a school section.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: But my grandfather, he got a homestead.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: 88. 88. That mother's little hand. It came in 1985. And the homesteads was all gone then, except the one that granddad got. His name was Jim Turpin. Irishman. His name was Jim Turpin. Turpin. Well, Turpin. Turpin. Jim Turpin. The homesteaded over there in that little five-acre patch right on the top hill past the hay barn up there. He had a little old soft shack there and had a fireplace in it for years and years there. Once in a while, you work a rock out for that fireplace. I haven't seen a rock there for 20 or 30 years. But most of the while, you scratch out a rock here.

speaker: Rob Moore

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It's called the Cherry Tree Hill. The first hill the other side of the Meyers Place. You go for real steep hills. Well, you go down and then up. Yeah. And up on top. That's Cherry Tree Hill. Yeah, that one used to be a little homestead right up there. And I dug rock out of there. One spring I went over there and worked for, I don't know, two or three days digging rock out of there. It was a, I figured it was a cellar. It was a foundation, you know. The rock, the top rock had all been hauled off. But this old foundation was around there and they kept working up. And they were good-sized rocks. Tucked like that, you know. Cyrus Roberts was telling me that one time he went down there. Cyrus' dad had one of the first mowing machines in the country. And the old fellow that lived there was by the name of Judd. And he went down there and did some mowing for Judd. And it came nice and Cyrus wasn't true, so they invited him to stay all night. And he stayed all night sleeping in the barn. And he said for supper, they didn't have any sweet corn. They had field corn. Young field corn, you know, worked out hard.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And... Oh, Judd had a brother-in-law that was staying with him. And he wasn't too bright. And Cyrus said that that guy, I don't know how many years of that field corn he ate. But all in the night, Cyrus woke up and that guy was a-rolling. He was sleeping in the barn with Cyrus. And he got up and he staggered around and he said he finally made it down to the house. And he hollered, he said, Judd! Hey, Judd!

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Judd, just what do you want?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He said, go ahead and shoot me, Judd! Get a goat and shoot me! He hates the much of that corn that he was in terrible misery. Yeah, Cyrus told me that one time. You'd take Cal that pulled down Harry Langdon's and I was working along the fence right there on top of the hill. And he'd come along and he told me that story. He told me who the guy was that lived there.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

Well, you see that the line fence here, you know where the line fence is between the Alsom Place and Andes. Well, that's the line fence that went right on north, you see. That would be pretty much where that road was. Yeah, that's where the A .J. was used to. Yeah, that's the same slide. And that would be a half a mile from there to the other end and a half a mile from there back to this end. There's a mile to Lincoln's Place.

I can see that one. Yeah, they had a fiddler sitting up there by a go. Dad and mother used to go to dad's, I don't know who the fiddler was now. I can see him sitting up there playing the fiddle upstairs over the greener there. It's a pretty good side hall. And then they built a church. The community went together and built a church. They come in with the church. And it wound up being a Methodist church. They told it. I don't know how they got de-toothed or anything, but it wound up in a Methodist church. But it was built as a community church. Anybody that's wanted to use it was welcome. It was the intention it was built with. And school, I don't know how many, well that school house that's down there now is the third school house that was built on that property. George has got the first one. Yeah, the old one was over here at Halins. The second one that tore down. And Frankie made a residence out of the third one. Yeah, the third one was partly built out of the second one. A lot of lumber in the third school house that was taken out of the second one. But the first one still intact.

words: They never celebrated. One of them celebrated before the 4th of July, the other didn't. Well, Juliaetta used to have a picnic. Some picnic deal was that. speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, just same as any two towns close together. Just rivalrous. Juliaetta always figured that Kendrick and all would always work on a baseball game if they could. Poor decisions. Rocking decisions. I don't know whether I ever told you or not, but I ran on to a guy up here at the... Hot Springs. They used to live down here on the Juliaetta grade as I got it. He lived where Stub Roberts used to live. Down the whole world. And it was either... I think that was the place. Anyway, they used to peddle produce. Garden produce, turn to Detroit and Moscow. And he knew all the guy's names along this road. Hell, he recognized my name right away. He did. Yeah. And he wanted to know if I lived on that road between Juliaetta and Troy. Anyway, he said he used to go up to Kendrick. He was just a kid then. And he said, the kids from Juliaetta used to go up to Kendrick and play a baseball. And he said, if we beat him, they'd run us over. He's down the railroad track with a baseball bat.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. I wish I could think of that guy's name. Probably the other way around too. But he remembered all those names up along there. Coxes and Woodies and Roberts and Arnie and right on up through. Allen and Harland, he could remember more.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah.

words: The Archers? Over the Archers?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. A lot of people go all day. I don't get it out for years.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Morsi's the only one left.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Mitchum used to use the audience about politics. Oh yeah, they were both Republicans, but they used to fight pretty often.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: The politics are pretty store point for some people. The people feel pretty strong. Oh, they took it pretty serious.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: From the time they had that night...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: They never argued over politics, did they?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, this one, they got into it. This one had a Republican rally down here in Gentry. Oh, and it was right after the town had burnt, and there was only one or two little shacks left in the town. And they were going to have a Republican rally, and they didn't let Granddad know about it. Oh, he was left out. I suppose old J .I. Mitchum worked it up. Yeah, he worked it up. And by golly Granddad heard about it, and when he heard about it, the meeting was in progress. And Sam, of course, went down. Just mattering with him, you know, because he hadn't been invited. And when he got to the place, why they was all in there, and J .I. was making a speech telling what an S-old mean to Sam was. Oh no! And Granddad walked in the door and stood there and heard him. And he made a die for it. He got old J .I. got his head under his arm like this. And the next day, the folks didn't know nothing about it. And they met old J .I. on the mayor hall, and he used to carry the mayor horseback in it. And he was going down by the old Johnson place by the old sea torches, you know, or the old Gordon orchard. And they met J .I. coming up horseback, and his face was... You could just see a little out of one eye, and his face was all covered with gauze and plastered up. He stopped to get his man, and Dad looked at that back, and he said, I'll be down with the boys! He didn't know that his Dad had worked him over. Yeah, he just grunted. He didn't go into detail. I happened to go downtown better, and what it took place. Oh, they had several fights.

speaker: Rob Moore

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter

words: Oh, hell, I can remember her old office... Get that picnic or a little jar and get you to have a picnic or something like that. Yeah, I can remember when they'd come down here and give a picture to the school office and the school office would be full. Guys like... Oh, who in the hell was that guy that was in the House of Representative Burton L.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I can remember him being down there, and I think old... Who was the Senator from...

speaker: Rob Moore

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I think La Borah even spoke down there. The old Borah. The Lion of Idaho they used to call him. And of course, the... Oh, the sheriffs and things like that from that county. The county officials. They'd come down here, you know, and speak at the... Now they just get on the air... I don't remember whether they're all Republicans or not. They didn't believe it. This county used to be a strong Republican. You couldn't elect anybody but a Republican. Democrat just well not run. He just well not run. But I don't know when it turned to tide with all about Franklin Roosevelt's administration. I think when the Democrats started to get in...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't Roosevelt have some pretty good programs for farmers?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, I thought he did. He started and they're still using a lot of it yet. He balanced my budget, and that's how to fight beans. There was a lot of Republicans holleting about it. They're still laying stuff to Roosevelt. And they holleted about the things that they were doing, and when the Republicans got in, they went right on to the program. They never knew the difference. Never knew the difference. They had to. They knew that... They knew if they didn't, they were out. It was a good program and it was what the people wanted, and if they intend to stay in business, they'd better go along with it. We never knew the difference from one administration to the other, except he got a little tougher all the time. They didn't know...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You know, when Roosevelt was elected, I remember old Wade King said to me, I said, no Wade?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I said, I don't. But I said, I think I will get four bits. And we did. And hell, it was only about 28 cents then. Wade was a good guy, but oh, he could be the most... Oh, he was a most obnoxious, goddamn politician. He was a rotten Republican. He laid everything to Roosevelt. He never... He hadn't forgot the Civil War. He was still fighting the Civil War, you know. He was always calling about the Civil War. I saw Wade one time. He was the maddest man. I think he was the maddest I ever saw Wade. I was hauling wheat, and they had that old 26th Chevrolet truck, and it was just a year or two after we'd had it. And Wade was hauling wheat for the company's right machine. He used to drive a grain tank. A town of horses. A town that I have this old Jimmy truck. And it was over there at the road state, I'm sure. We used to always haul first. It never didn't like to work. The Traveling Man. Just happened to be a long Traveling Man, and I don't know how it happened, but Wade, when I drove up there, him and Wade was in an argument. And he was just as rabid a Democrat as Wade was Republican. He was smart. This Traveling Man was well posted. And by the time the Traveling Man was making it pretty rough on Wade, finally he just got disgusted and... Turned around to leave Wade, and as he started to leave, he said, you don't know enough about what you're talking about to ask questions.

words: Boy, I'm talking about being mad.
